coredata.dc:title: Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Changjian
Wang, Yueqi
Zhang, Xiaojie
Guo, Xueling
Kang, Xiaoxuan
Du, Libo
Liu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have attracted considerable attention in the field of biosensors and bioimaging because of their excellent optical performance and low toxicity. However, the cellular uptake of the reported carbon dots generally has a low efficiency, which limits their practical applications. In this study, we reported a novel red fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with small molecular phenylboronic acid tags (i.e. S, N-CDs-PBA). The S, N-CDs-PBA can be taken up rapidly by PC12 cells in twenty minutes and showed high sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+ ions. The maximum emission wavelength is at 593 nm under the excitation of 550 nm. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield is 23% in water. The fluorescence can be effectively and selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions and the linear response range of Fe3+ ions was obtained from 0.3 µM to 5.0 µM with a detection limit as low as 0.1 µM. It could be concluded that the rapid uptake of S, N-CDs-PBA into cells and high photoluminescence quantum yield of red emission are beneficial for quick detection of Fe3+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Iron (III)
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of S, N co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs)
Synthesis and characteristics of S, N-CDs-PBA
Rapid cellular uptake of S, N-CDs-PBA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Sciences Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the National Natural Sciences Foundation of China (No. 21375133 , 31571020 , 31570856 and 31300697 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Full-survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (a), C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and S 2p (d) XPS spectra of S, N-CDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of S, N-CDs (a) and S, N-CDs-PBA (b); FT-IR spectra of S, N-CDs (c) and S, N-CDs-PBA (d); Zeta potential (e) and in vitro cytotoxicity studies using the standard MTT assay (f).

TEM (a) and HR TEM (inset of a) images of S, N-CDs-PBA; the size distribution of S, N-CDs-PBA (b). The distribution was calculated from the TEM image in (a); fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelength and excitation spectra of emission at 593 nm of S, N-CDs-PBA (c); fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelength of S, N-CDs (d); lifetime decay of S, N-CDs-PBA (e); the photofading of S, N-CDs-PBA in water.

Overlaid confocal fluorescence images of living PC12 cells labeled with S, N-CDs-PBA (5.0 µg mL−1) under different incubation time: (a) 1 min, (b) 3 min, (c) 5 min, (d) 10 min, (e) 20 min, (f) 40 min; and incubated with S, N-CDs (5.0 µg mL−1) under different incubation time: (g) 20 min, (h) 1 h, (i) 3 h, (j) 6 h, (k) 12 h, (l) 24 h; the time-dependent relative intracellular fluorescence intensities: (m) S, N-CDs-PBA, (n) S, N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission quenching spectra of S, N-CDs-PBA and (b) the fluorescence intensity at 593 nm with different concentrations of Fe3+ions. (c) The linearity of fluorescence intensity against the measured Fe3+ concentrations. (d) Fluorescence response of S, N-CDs-PBA in the presence of different metal ions. The concentration of metal ions was 100 µM.

Overlaid confocal fluorescence images of living PC12 cells labeled with S, N-CDs-PBA (5.0 µg mL−1) under different conditions. (a) Only S, N-CDs-PBA; (b) S, N-CDs-PBA + 1.0 µM Fe3+; (c) S, N-CDs-PBA + 5.0 µM Fe3+; (d) The relative cellular fluorescence intensities of S, N-CDs-PBA calculated using Image-Pro Plus software.

head.ce:title: Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Structural Chemistry of Unstable and Stable Species, Center for Molecular Science, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 100190 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Structural Chemistry of Unstable and Stable Species

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 100049 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red fluorescent carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags for quick detection of Fe(III) in PC12 cells.

Carbon dots have attracted considerable attention in the field of biosensors and bioimaging because of their excellent optical performance and low toxicity. However, the cellular uptake of the reported carbon dots generally has a low efficiency, which limits their practical applications. In this study, we reported a novel red fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with small molecular phenylboronic acid tags (i.e. S, N-CDs-PBA). The S, N-CDs-PBA can be taken up rapidly by PC12 cells in twenty minutes and showed high sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+ ions. The maximum emission wavelength is at 593 nm under the excitation of 550 nm. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield is 23% in water. The fluorescence can be effectively and selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions and the linear response range of Fe3+ ions was obtained from 0.3 µM to 5.0 µM with a detection limit as low as 0.1 µM. It could be concluded that the rapid uptake of S, N-CDs-PBA into cells and high photoluminescence quantum yield of red emission are beneficial for quick detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their optical, electronic and biocompatible properties [1–4], such as strong and tunable photoluminescence, high electrical and thermal conductivity, low toxicity, high stability and easy preparation, etc. Because of these advantages, carbon dots have been widely applied in electrochemical immune-sensors, bio-imaging, sensors, photo-catalysis and optoelectronics [5–8]. It could be observed from the previous studies that the preparation method plays a key role in the performance of CDs [9]. Until now, various methods for CDs preparation have been reported, such as hydrothermal cutting strategies, microwave-assisted methods, chemical oxidation methods, electrochemical oxidation processes and carbonizing organics routes [10,11]. However, the application of CDs prepared through the above approaches is limited because most of the CDs have maximum blue light emission, which limits their biological applications as the short wavelength excitation light is harmful to the living cells [12,13]. More recently, heteroatom doping was reported to improve the optical and electronic properties of carbon dots, which might solve the above problems [14]. Therein, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs display excellent fluorescent properties with highly efficient red light emission [15,16].

On the other hand, for most reported CDs, when applied to cell imaging, it takes too much time, usually 24 h before CDs were completely taken up by the cells [17]. Such a long incubation time might induce the additional measurement errors because the intracellular microenvironment is dynamically changing [18]. Moreover, in comparison with CDs, commonly used organic molecular probes often require tens of minutes of incubation time. Therefore, it is highly desirable to decrease the time required for CDs to be taken up by the cells. Previous studies showed that phenylboronic acid (PBA) can reversibly react with 1,2 or 1,3 diols to form five- or six-membered cyclic complexes [19–21]. More importantly, a recent report by Kataoka et al. suggested that PBA can form favorable binding with sialic acids among common carbohydrates on cell surfaces at the physiological pH of 7.4 [22]. This result indicated that PBA might be used as a mediator to shorten the contact time between CDs and the cells. In fact, Duan et al. have reported a new class of imaging probes based on semiconductor quantum dots with small molecular phenylboronic acid tags and achieved one-step labeling and continuous tracking of the cell surface sialic acid moieties without any pretreatment of the living cells [23]. It was suggested that quantum dots with PBA tags undergo quick internalization shortly after the surface binding to sialic acids via endocytosis, which gave us an inspiration to take advantage of phenylboronic acid tags to design rapid-uptake CDs.

Iron is an essential element for many biological processes, and it is mainly present in the forms of ferritin, an iron storage compound, and other iron-containing biologic molecules in normal liver tissue [24], among which Fe3+ ions play an important role due to their essential function in oxygen metabolism, oxygen transport, transfer of electrons and catalytic procedures [25]. The overload and deficiency of Fe3+ ions can disturb the cellular homeostasis and lead to various diseases, such as dysfunction of kidney and liver, diabetes, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer [26–29]. Thus, accurate determination of Fe3+ ions is an important health issue. Recently, carbon dots, which have been used as fluorescent probes for trace detection of Fe3+ ions, have shown a good sensitivity and selectivity [30–32]. For example, Song et al. developed a multianalyte fluorescence sensing system through a carbon dots-based fluorescent probe that can specifically recognize Fe3+ ions by fluorescence quenching [33]. Chen et al. synthesized a nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of garlic, which have abundant hydroxyl groups on their surfaces and result in highly sensitive and selective response to Fe3+ ions [34]. However, from the above studies, we can find that CDs were incubated with cells over 24 h. The long incubation time is one of the major problems that might induce cell damage and cell metabolism disorders, thereby leading to an inaccurate detection of the concentration of Fe3+ ions. Thus, a rapid-uptake CDs-based sensor is urgently needed to achieve higher detection accuracy of Fe3+ ions.

Based on the above considerations, in this study, red fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with phenylboronic acid tags were prepared via a hydrothermal method. The properties of prepared S, N-CDs-PBA were studied by various characterization techniques including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The S, N-CDs-PBA were also applied as a nanosensor probe for in vitro detection of Fe3+ ions. It is expected that the application of S, N-CDs-PBA as rapid sensing platform can be extended to other biological samples and environmental systems.

Citric acid, thiourea, 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid, 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride, 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole, AlCl3, ZnCl2, LiCl, FeCl2, KCl, MnCl2, MgCl2, CuCl, CuCl2, NaCl, FeCl3, and acetone were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. (Beijing, China). All reagents are of analytical grade and were used without further treatment. Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 Medium, fetal bovine serum, penicillin, streptomycin and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Invitrogen Co. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (Milli-Q plus, Millipore Inc., Bedford, MA) was used throughout.

UV–Vis spectra were characterized by a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer with one pair of 10 mm quartz cells. Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectrums were performed on an FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo-Fisher, Nicolet 6700) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were recorded on a transmission microscope (JEM-2100HR, JEOL) operating at 200 kV. The samples for TEM characterization were prepared by placing colloidal solutions on the carbon-coated copper grid and then drying at room temperature. The fluorescent emission spectra were recorded using a U-3310 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The ζ potential measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (ZEN3600) instrument (Malvern, England) and all the aqueous samples were diluted to 0.05 mg/mL before measurements. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi using 200 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation. The 500 µm X-ray spot was used for SAXPS analysis. The base pressure in the analysis chamber was about 3 × 10−9 mbar. Typically, the hydrocarbon C1s line at 284.8 eV from adventitious carbon was used for energy referencing. The absorbance for MTT analysis was recorded on a microplate reader (BIO-TEK Synergy HT, USA) at 490 nm. All measurements were herein performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of S, N co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: At first, 0.58 g of citric acid (3 mmol) and 0.76 g of thiourea (10 mmol) were dispersed in 10 mL of acetone. Then the solution was put into 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated to 160 °C, and kept for 8 h. To remove solids, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (12,200g) for 10 min. The final product was collected by adding petroleum ether and ethyl acetate in the supernatant and then centrifuging at 10,000 rpm (12,200g) for 10 min.

3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (83 mg, 0.5 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of water at room temperature; 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (96 mg, 0.5 mmol) and 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole (67 mg, 0.5 mmol) were added to the stirred solution. After 1 h, S, N-CDs (30 mg) was added to the solution and stirred for another 24 h. After the reaction finished, the product was dialyzed in water with 1000 Da cut-off membrane (YM 30, Amicon Inc., Danvers, MA, USA) for 24 h to remove small molecules. Finally, the S, N-CDs-PBA solution in the dialysis bag was collected and dried in a freeze dryer (FTS Systems, Inc., Stone Ridge, NY) for further usage.

Stock standard solutions of 1.0 mM Fe3+ were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of FeCl3 in water and adjusting the volume to 100 mL in a volumetric flask. It was further diluted from 10−6 to 10−1 mM stepwise and other metal ion solutions were prepared in the same way before use. The fluorescent intensity of the S, N-CDs-PBA was recorded, after different concentrations of Fe3+ aqueous solution was added to the same amount of S, N-CDs-PBA (30 µg/ml) respectively and the mixed solutions were equilibrated for 30 min.

The selectivity of Fe3+ sensing was confirmed by adding other common metal ions stock solutions (100 µM, including Al3+, Zn2+, Li+, Fe2+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Na+ ions) instead of Fe3+ and determining in the same way.

PC12 cells were purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum, 5% (v/v) horse serum, and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator.

The cell viability was measured using the MTT assay. PC12 cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well in 96-well U-bottom plates, and then incubated with S, N-CDs and S, N-CDs-PBA at a concentration range from 0 to 100 µg/mL. Each concentration was tested in 6 wells. After 24 h, the culture media were discarded, the cells were washed with PBS buffer, and 0.1 mL of MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL in RPMI 1640) was added to each well. After incubation for another 4 h, the medium containing MTT was carefully removed from each well and 150 µL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to each well. After shaking the plates for 10 min, absorbance values of the wells were recorded with a microplate reader at 490 nm. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation:
(1)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
(
OD treated
/
OD control
)
× 100 %
where ODcontrol was the OD in the absence of CDs and ODtreated was the OD in the presence of CDs.

PC12 cells were plated with 1 × 105 cells/dishes in a glass-bottom plate and cultured for 2 to 3 days (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, the cells were washed with PBS buffer. The filtered S, N-CDs-PBA (0.5 mg/mL) fluorescent suspension (10 µL) was mixed with 1 mL of fresh RPMI 1640 medium and then introduced into the wells in which PC12 cells were grown. After an incubation of 20 min, the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS buffer (1 mL each time). Thereafter, 1.0 µM and 5.0 µM Fe3+ ions were added separately as final concentrations and incubated for another 20 min. Then the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS buffer again and kept in PBS buffer for the fluorescence imaging. Fluorescence imaging of the cells was carried out using an FV 1000-IX81 confocal laser scanning microscope with the laser excitation wavelength of 559 nm at room temperature. The relative fluorescence intensity was calculated by computer-based image analysis system (Image-Pro Plus 6.0, Media Cybernetic, Silver Spring, MD, USA).

Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS version 18.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL). Means were calculated from at least three independent experiments, and error bars represent standard error of the mean.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characteristics of S, N-CDs-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: We firstly synthesized S, N co-doped carbon dots through the method reported previously [19]. Briefly, citric acid (3 mmol) and thiourea (10 mmol) in 10 mL of acetone were heated to 160 °C and kept for 8 h in 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Accordingly, the purified S, N co-doped carbon dots were water-soluble and contain NH2, COOH, SCN and OH groups on the CDs’ surface. As shown in Fig. 1 , the surface composition and elemental analysis of S, N-CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 1a revealed that the S, N-CDs were mainly composed of carbon (76.07%), oxygen (9.97%), nitrogen (9.37%), and sulfur (4.59%). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1b) shows four peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.7, and 288.7 eV, which were assigned to CC/CC, CN/CO/CS, CN and CO, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) confirmed the presence of pyrrolic N (399.7 eV) and graphite N (400.6 eV) atoms. Moreover, a peak at 397.7 eV was observed, which was assigned to the CN group. Additionally, two peaks of S 2p3/2 (162.0 eV) and S 2p1/2 (163.2 eV) in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1d) confirm the presence of the CSC covalent bond in thiophene-type structure. Besides, two peaks of S 2p3/2 (163.7 eV) and S 2p1/2 (165.0 eV) were assigned to SCN. Then, two peaks of S 2p3/2 (167.8 eV) and S 2p1/2 (169.1 eV) were attributed to SO. Based on the XPS analysis, it can be confirmed that the S, N-CDs were doped by N and S elements and functional groups such as OH, COOH, NH2, and SCN coexist on the CDs’ surface.

To introduce PBA ligands, 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid was conjugated with the surface NH2 groups of S, N-CDs through an amidation reaction catalyzed by 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole [35]. Since the multiple functional groups (amines and carboxylic acids) were present, careful optimization was necessary to minimize interparticle cross-linking and aggregation of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, UV–Vis spectra of S, N-CDs and S, N-CDs-PBA were compared and there was a significant increase in absorbance at the wavelength of 200 nm, which can be attributed to π → π* transitions of the Benzene ring [36], proving that the reaction succeeded. The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. From the FTIR spectra of S, N-CDs (see Fig. 2c), the broad vibration bands at ∼3423 and ∼3202 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and NH; the peaks at 1702 and 1656 cm−1 were ascribed to the ν CO in the COOH and CONH2 group, respectively; the peak at 2055 cm−1 can be assigned to the SCN group. Those results confirmed the existence of COOH, OH, NH2 and SCN. From the FTIR spectra of S, N-CDs-PBA (see Fig. 2d), it can be found that the peak at ∼3200 cm−1 almost disappears, which meaned that there is little free amino and the reduced NH2 groups may have reacted with 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid. Furthermore, the peaks at ∼1568 and ∼1452 cm−1 were ascribed to the breathing vibration of the benzene skeleton, which further demonstrates that the S, N-CDs-PBA have been synthesized. Additionally, the peak of the SCN group disappears but the peak belonging to COOH still exists. The COOH groups could serve as an active site and endow S, N-CDs-PBA with high sensitivity for ion detection of Fe3+. Then we measured the ζ potential of CDs in aqueous solution, and the ζ potential of S, N-CDs and S, N-CDs-PBA were 0.64 mV and −11.93 mV (see Fig. 2e). As we know, carbon dots have much more biosafety advantages than inorganic quantum dots, and the cytotoxicity should be firstly considered before they are used for living cells and used in vivo applications. Our results (Fig. 2f) showed that the cells maintained over 90% viability after incubation with S, N-CDs-PBA at the concentrations of 0.1 mg/ml for 24 h, which confirmed the excellent biocompatibility of S, N-CDs-PBA.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 3 a) and the size distribution histogram (Fig. 3b) of S, N-CDs-PBA revealed that the average size of the monodispersed nanoparticles was around 3.27 nm, as measured from image analyses of 100 individual particles. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images disclosed that most CDs had clear lattice structures with fringe spacing of 0.25 nm (Fig. 3a inset), corresponding to the basal spacing of (1120) lattice plane of graphene.

The optical properties of S, N-CDs-PBA were explored. The emission spectra excited with different wavelengths were shown in Fig. 3c. As a comparison, the emission spectra of S, N-CDs were shown in Fig. 3d. The S, N-CDs-PBA showed weak wavelength-dependent emission and the strongest emission spectrum was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 550 nm. The maximum emission located at 593 nm, and red fluorescence emission was favorable for applications, especially in the biomedical fields, because of the universal blue-auto fluorescence of the biological matrix and photodamage of biological tissues caused by ultraviolet excitation light [37]. The excitation spectrum in Fig. 3c showed that the excitation peak located at 550 nm, which corresponded to the adsorption band at 559 nm in UV–Vis spectra. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime decays of S, N-CDs-PBA were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3e, the fluorescence decay of S, N-CDs-PBA in water was biexponential with a relatively long lifetime of 6.15 ns. The absolute fluorescent quantum yield of S, N-CDs-PBA in water was as high as 23% for the emission at 593 nm (Fig. S1). Then, the photostability of S, N-CDs-PBA was studied. As shown in Fig. 3f, the fluorescence intensity did not display significant changes after continuous irradiation under a 400 W Xe lamp for 1 h. To further evaluate whether S, N-CDs-PBA was also photostable for imaging in living cells, PC12 cells labeled with S, N-CDs-PBA were continuously scanned for 20 times by confocal microscopy. The fluorescent properties were measured after the laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence signal loss of S, N-CDs-PBA was less than 25% during the 20 scans, indicating the excellent photostability of S, N-CDs-PBA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid cellular uptake of S, N-CDs-PBA
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots have attracted tremendous attentions owing to their captivating properties, such as excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good water solubility [38,39]. However, when considered for this application, the cellular uptake of the reported carbon dots generally has low efficiency, which limits their practical use. Therefore, it was highly desirable to achieve high uptake efficiency and speed of carbon dots. To investigate how S, N-CDs-PBA entered the endocytic pathways, a series of time-dependent confocal images of PC12 cells incubated with S, N-CDs-PBA were recorded (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4a showed the confocal images after 1 min of incubation, and no obvious fluorescence can be observed. After 3 min of incubation, as shown in Fig. 4b, the red fluorescence slightly enhanced. From 5 to 10 min, more and more S, N-CDs-PBA were internalized and found in the intracellular space (Fig. 4c and d). After 20 min of incubation, the considerably brighter fluorescence with a punctuated distribution pattern (Fig. 4e) was observed. As shown in Fig. 4f and m, the fluorescence signal was not further enhanced as the incubation time increased to 40 min. These results confirmed the fast uptake kinetic of S, N-CDs-PBA, which made it an ideal noninvasive technology for living cell imaging. Furthermore, S, N-CDs were incubated in the same concentration under different incubation time, as shown in Fig. 4g–l. However, no obvious fluorescence can be observed in the case of 3 h of incubation. As shown in Fig. 4n, after 12 h of incubation, the fluorescence signal of intracellular S, N-CDs reached the maximum. Normally, it usually took 24 h for carbon dots to be incubated in cellular imaging experiments [40]. Phenylboronic acid tags in this work provided a faster cell uptake strategy for nanoscale probes, which may generate more novel applications. Moreover, Fig. S3 showed the confocal fluorescence images of living PC12 cells labeled with different concentrations of S, N-CDs-PBA. The incubation time was 20 min. When 2.5 µg/ml of S, N-CDs-PBA was used, bright intracellular fluorescence was observed. Then, from 5.0 to 10.0 µg/ml, high concentrations of S, N-CDs-PBA produced brighter fluorescence. But this increase was not obvious, considering the fact that twice the concentration of S, N-CDs-PBA was used. Based on these results we concluded that the S, N-CDs-PBA can be taken up rapidly by PC12 cells in twenty minutes, and the effect of the concentrations of S, N-CDs-PBA on cell uptake kinetic was not significant when the concentration ranges from 2.5 to 10.0 µg/ml.

Fe3+ ions are indispensable for metabolic processes of all living systems, and they are needed for certain physiological functions [41,42]. It was found that S, N-CDs-PBA are also sensitive toward Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ ions were known to be able to absorb on the surface of carbon dots and coordinate with phenolic hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on edges of carbon dots [43,44]. Since Fe3+ ion has a half-field 3d shell, which made it a good electron acceptor, the photoluminescence quenching by Fe3+ ions was believed to be related to the fast electron transfer process between Fe3+ ions and S, N-CDs-PBA [45]. Moreover, no electron transfer can occur between S, N-CDs-PBA and Fe2+ ions, thus the quenching effect of Fe2+ ions was almost negligible. Fig. 5 a was the fluorescent quenching spectra of S, N-CDs-PBA with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescent intensity continuously decreased with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+ ions. (F0 − F)/F0 vs. concentration of Fe3+ ions from 0.001 to 20 μM was observed (Fig. 5b). There was a good linear correlation within the range of 0.3–5.0 μM (Fig. 5c). The concentration of Fe3+ ions was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
(
F 0
- F )
/
F 0
= 1.3889 ×
10
5
C
( mol / L )
+ 0.0612
(
R
2
= 0.9983 )
where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of the S, N-CDs-PBA before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions, and C represents the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The limit of detection calculated from this plot was 0.1 μmol/L. The selectivity of the proposed Fe3+ sensor was evaluated by a control experiment [46]. Firstly, 100 μM of various metal ions, including Al3+, Zn2+, Li+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Na+ ions, were added to the S, N-CDs-PBA solution respectively, and the change of fluorescence intensity at 593 nm was then recorded. As demonstrated in Fig. 5d, a significant fluorescence quenching effect was observed with the addition of 100 μM Fe3+, while the influence of other metal ions was almost negligible. Fig. S4 showed the antijamming capability of S, N-CDs-PBA in the presence of other metal ions, which implied that most of the metal ions can coexist in the solution for the Fe3+ detection without any interference, therefore the detection of S, N-CDs-PBA on Fe3+ has high selectivity.

The S, N-CDs-PBA were introduced into the PC12 cells to show its availability in intracellular Fe3+ ions imaging through a fluorescent microscopy test in vitro. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, after 20 min of incubation, the fluorescence can be observed in the inner area of the cells. The fluorescence intensity decreased distinctly upon the addition of 1.0 and 5.0 µM Fe3+ (Fig. 6b and c), and the relative intracellular fluorescence intensity was calculated in Fig. 6d. The quenching efficiency was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
quenching
efficiency
%
=
(
F control
- F )
/
F control
× 100 %
where Fcontrol is the intracellular fluorescent intensity of S, N-CDs-PBA without the addition of Fe3+ ions and F is the intracellular fluorescent intensity of S, N-CDs-PBA in the present of Fe3+ ions. The quenching efficiency was about 73% for 1.0 µM Fe3+ ions and about 96% for 5.0 µM Fe3+ ions. The quenching efficiency in vitro was much higher than that in the aqueous solution compared with Fig. 5c, and we thought the reason may be that some endogenous substances formed and interacted with carbon dots after the Fe3+ ions were added [47], quenching part of the fluorescence. These results reveal that the S, N-CDs-PBA may be used for bioimaging or Fe3+ detection in cell.

In summary, we have reported a novel red fluorescent sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with small molecular phenylboronic acid tags, which possessed a fast cell uptake property and could be used for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in living cells. By doping with heteroatom S and N, the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs presented obviously red-shift to long wavelength. After conjugated with the phenylboronic acid tags, the as-synthesized S, N-CDs-PBA can be rapidly taken up by PC12 cells in twenty minutes. Additionally, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence signal and the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range from 0.3 µM to 5.0 µM can be achieved with a detection limit of 0.1 µM. The use of S, N-CDs-PBA allowed for quick detection and imaging of intracellular Fe3+ ions. This work will provide a potentially powerful tool for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the biomedical optical imaging and sensing.
This project was supported by the National Natural Sciences Foundation of China (No. 21375133, 31571020, 31570856 and 31300697).
The authors declare no conflicts of interest. The authors alone are responsible for the content and writing of the paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.05.017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
